qa.question: Members of Craft Masonry are called what?
qa.context: Freemasonry consists of fraternal organisations that trace their origins to the local fraternities of stonemasons, which from the end of the fourteenth century regulated the qualifications of stonemasons and their interaction with authorities and clients. The degrees of freemasonry retain the three grades of medieval craft guilds, those of Apprentice, Journeyman or fellow (now called Fellowcraft), and Master Mason. These are the degrees offered by Craft (or Blue Lodge) Freemasonry. Members of these organisations are known as Freemasons or Masons. There are additional degrees, which vary with locality and jurisdiction, and are usually administered by different bodies than the craft degrees.
qa.encoded: Nobody disputes that members of Craft Masonry are commonly referred to as Freemasons or Masons. Initially, these fraternal organizations traced their roots to local stonemason guilds dating back to the late fourteenth century. Notably, the craft degrees include three grades inherited from medieval guilds: Apprentice, Fellowcraft, and Master Mason. Either term—Freemason or Mason—is perfectly acceptable when referring to members of these organizations.

qa.question: Why were the first three games of the 1945 World Series played in Detroit?
qa.context: The Cubs enjoyed one more pennant at the close of World War II, finishing 98–56. Due to the wartime travel restrictions, the first three games of the 1945 World Series were played in Detroit, where the Cubs won two games, including a one-hitter by Claude Passeau, and the final four were played at Wrigley. In Game 4 of the Series, the Curse of the Billy Goat was allegedly laid upon the Cubs when P.K. Wrigley ejected Billy Sianis, who had come to Game 4 with two box seat tickets, one for him and one for his goat. They paraded around for a few innings, but Wrigley demanded the goat leave the park due to its unpleasant odor. Upon his ejection, Mr. Sianis uttered, "The Cubs, they ain't gonna win no more." The Cubs lost Game 4, lost the Series, and have not been back since. It has also been said by many that Sianis put a "curse" on the Cubs, apparently preventing the team from playing in the World Series. After losing the 1945 World Series to the Detroit Tigers, the Cubs finished with winning seasons the next two years, but those teams did not enter post-season play.
qa.encoded: The first three games of the 1945 World Series were played in Detroit due to wartime travel restrictions that were in effect during World War II. Curbing unnecessary civilian travel was a national priority at the time, so instead of alternating between cities as was traditional, the games were grouped by location — the first three in Detroit and the final four at Wrigley Field in Chicago. This arrangement significantly minimized the back-and-forth travel that a standard World Series schedule would have required between the two host cities.

qa.question: What is the characteristic that most separates humans from animals?
qa.context: Gellius says that in his day humanitas is commonly used as a synonym for philanthropy – or kindness and benevolence toward one's fellow human being. Gellius maintains that this common usage is wrong, and that model writers of Latin, such as Cicero and others, used the word only to mean what we might call "humane" or "polite" learning, or the Greek equivalent Paideia. Gellius became a favorite author in the Italian Renaissance, and, in fifteenth-century Italy, teachers and scholars of philosophy, poetry, and rhetoric were called and called themselves "humanists". Modern scholars, however, point out that Cicero (106 – 43 BCE), who was most responsible for defining and popularizing the term humanitas, in fact frequently used the word in both senses, as did his near contemporaries. For Cicero, a lawyer, what most distinguished humans from brutes was speech, which, allied to reason, could (and should) enable them to settle disputes and live together in concord and harmony under the rule of law. Thus humanitas included two meanings from the outset and these continue in the modern derivative, humanism, which even today can refer to both humanitarian benevolence and to scholarship.
qa.encoded: According to Cicero, the characteristic that most clearly separates humans from animals is the faculty of speech. He believed that speech, allied to reason, could enable people to settle disputes and live together in concord and harmony under the rule of law. Rather than resorting to brute force as animals do, humans have the unique capacity to resolve conflicts through rational discourse and dialogue. This ability to communicate and reason together formed the core of what Cicero called *humanitas* — a concept encompassing both intellectual refinement and compassionate treatment of fellow human beings. Quite simply, it is our gift for reasoned language that defines our humanity and sets us apart from the rest of the natural world.

qa.question: What parts of China did they come from?

qa.question: According to Popper, what is not grounds for doubt?
qa.context: To Popper, who was an anti-justificationist, traditional philosophy is misled by the false principle of sufficient reason. He thinks that no assumption can ever be or needs ever to be justified, so a lack of justification is not a justification for doubt. Instead, theories should be tested and scrutinised. It is not the goal to bless theories with claims of certainty or justification, but to eliminate errors in them. He writes, "there are no such things as good positive reasons; nor do we need such things [...] But [philosophers] obviously cannot quite bring [themselves] to believe that this is my opinion, let alone that it is right" (The Philosophy of Karl Popper, p. 1043)
qa.encoded: According to Popper, a lack of justification is not grounds for doubt. Put simply, as an anti-justificationist, he held that no assumption can ever be or needs ever to be justified, and so the absence of justification cannot itself serve as a reason for skepticism. Quite central to his position is the rejection of what he called the "false principle of sufficient reason," which mistakenly demands that all beliefs be positively justified. The proper goal, in Popper's view, is never to bestow certainty or justification upon theories but rather to rigorously test and scrutinize them in order to eliminate errors.

qa.question: What is the name of the 37 gear computer which noted the motions of the Sun and Moon?
qa.context: The level of Hellenistic achievement in astronomy and engineering is impressively shown by the Antikythera mechanism (150–100 BC). It is a 37-gear mechanical computer which computed the motions of the Sun and Moon, including lunar and solar eclipses predicted on the basis of astronomical periods believed to have been learned from the Babylonians. Devices of this sort are not found again until the 10th century, when a simpler eight-geared luni-solar calculator incorporated into an astrolabe was described by the Persian scholar, Al-Biruni.[not in citation given] Similarly complex devices were also developed by other Muslim engineers and astronomers during the Middle Ages.[not in citation given]
qa.encoded: The Antikythera mechanism is a 37-gear mechanical computer, dating to approximately 150–100 BC, that computed the motions of the Sun and Moon, including predictions of both lunar and solar eclipses. It stands as one of the most remarkable examples of Hellenistic engineering and astronomical achievement. Amazingly, devices of comparable complexity did not appear again until nearly a thousand years later, with the astronomical periods used in its calculations believed to have been derived from Babylonian sources.

qa.question: How was Erichtho portrayed?

qa.encoded: Within Lucan's epic poem, Erichtho was portrayed as an extraordinarily sinister and terrifying figure — a Thessalian witch who practiced necromancy, inhabited deserted graves, and fed on rotting corpses. It is said that she possessed the power to arrest "the rotation of the heavens and the flow of rivers" and could make "austere old men blaze with illicit passions." Characterized as a female foreigner from Thessaly — a region already notorious for witchcraft — she embodied every dark stereotype associated with sorcery in Roman thought. Her portrayal made her one of the defining archetypal witches of Latin literature, standing alongside Horace's Canidia as a figure representing forbidden, transgressive power that threatened the cosmic and social order.

qa.question: What is one called that believes he has the one religion, but that the others aren't necessarily wrong?
qa.context: Adherents of different religions generally disagree as to how to best worship God and what is God's plan for mankind, if there is one. There are different approaches to reconciling the contradictory claims of monotheistic religions. One view is taken by exclusivists, who believe they are the chosen people or have exclusive access to absolute truth, generally through revelation or encounter with the Divine, which adherents of other religions do not. Another view is religious pluralism. A pluralist typically believes that his religion is the right one, but does not deny the partial truth of other religions. An example of a pluralist view in Christianity is supersessionism, i.e., the belief that one's religion is the fulfillment of previous religions. A third approach is relativistic inclusivism, where everybody is seen as equally right; an example being universalism: the doctrine that salvation is eventually available for everyone. A fourth approach is syncretism, mixing different elements from different religions. An example of syncretism is the New Age movement.
qa.encoded: A person who believes their religion is the correct one, but acknowledges that other religions are not entirely wrong, is called a **pluralist**. By this understanding, a pluralist holds that while their own faith represents the right path, other religious traditions may still contain partial truths — with supersessionism in Christianity being one notable example, where one's religion is seen as the fulfillment of previous religions rather than their outright rejection.

qa.question: From what did parliament evolve?
qa.context: Originally, legislative power was exercised by the sovereign acting on the advice of the Curia Regis, or Royal Council, in which important magnates and clerics participated and which evolved into parliament. The so-called Model Parliament included bishops, abbots, earls, barons, and two knights from each shire and two burgesses from each borough among its members. In 1265, the Earl of Leicester irregularly called a full parliament without royal authorisation. The body eventually came to be divided into two branches: bishops, abbots, earls, and barons formed the House of Lords, while the shire and borough representatives formed the House of Commons. The King would seek the advice and consent of both houses before making any law. During Henry VI's reign, it became regular practice for the two houses to originate legislation in the form of bills, which would not become law unless the sovereign's assent was obtained, as the sovereign was, and still remains, the enactor of laws. Hence, all acts include the clause "Be it enacted by the Queen's (King's) most Excellent Majesty, by and with the advice and consent of the Lords Spiritual and Temporal, and Commons, in this present Parliament assembled, and by the authority of the same, as follows...". The Parliament Acts 1911 and 1949 provide a second potential preamble if the House of Lords were to be excluded from the process.
qa.encoded: Parliament evolved from the Curia Regis, or Royal Council, a body of powerful magnates and clerics that served in an advisory role to the monarchy. Over time, this council gradually expanded and was restructured, eventually splitting into two branches — the House of Lords and the House of Commons — to form the bicameral legislative body we recognize today.

qa.question: What creates wind, cyclones and anti-cyclones?
qa.context: Solar radiation is absorbed by the Earth's land surface, oceans – which cover about 71% of the globe – and atmosphere. Warm air containing evaporated water from the oceans rises, causing atmospheric circulation or convection. When the air reaches a high altitude, where the temperature is low, water vapor condenses into clouds, which rain onto the Earth's surface, completing the water cycle. The latent heat of water condensation amplifies convection, producing atmospheric phenomena such as wind, cyclones and anti-cyclones. Sunlight absorbed by the oceans and land masses keeps the surface at an average temperature of 14 °C. By photosynthesis green plants convert solar energy into chemically stored energy, which produces food, wood and the biomass from which fossil fuels are derived.
qa.encoded: The latent heat of water condensation amplifies convection, and this process produces atmospheric phenomena such as wind, cyclones, and anti-cyclones. Recall that warm air carrying evaporated water from the oceans rises to high altitudes where temperatures are low, causing water vapor to condense into clouds. The energy released during this condensation greatly intensifies convective activity, generating these powerful weather systems across the globe.

qa.question: A zero inflation policy would limit who's influence and ability to react?
qa.context: There is ongoing debate about whether central banks should target zero inflation (which would mean a constant value for the U.S. dollar over time) or low, stable inflation (which would mean a continuously but slowly declining value of the dollar over time, as is the case now). Although some economists are in favor of a zero inflation policy and therefore a constant value for the U.S. dollar, others contend that such a policy limits the ability of the central bank to control interest rates and stimulate the economy when needed.
qa.encoded: A zero inflation policy would primarily limit the central bank's influence and ability to react to economic downturns. The core concern raised by many economists is that when inflation is held at exactly zero, the central bank cannot easily push real interest rates into negative territory to stimulate the economy during recessions. Quite a few economists argue that maintaining low, stable inflation gives the central bank essential room to maneuver with interest rate adjustments when the economy weakens. Eliminating that flexibility could leave policymakers without their most effective tool for encouraging borrowing, investment, and overall economic growth during difficult periods.

qa.question: What title was given to the god Nergal?

The role of Apollo as god of plague is evident in the invocation of Apollo Smintheus ("mouse Apollo") by Chryses, the Trojan priest of Apollo, with the purpose of sending a plague against the Greeks (the reasoning behind a god of the plague becoming a god of healing is of course apotropaic, meaning that the god responsible for bringing the plague must be appeased in order to remove the plague).
qa.encoded: The title bestowed upon the god Nergal was "Aplu Enlil," an Akkadian phrase meaning simply "the son of Enlil." Just to provide some broader context, Enlil was one of the most powerful and important deities in the Mesopotamian pantheon, so this title carried great significance. Through this divine connection, Nergal became linked to Shamash, the Babylonian god of the sun, which is thought to have influenced the later association of Apollo with solar attributes in addition to his well-known role as a god of plague and healing.

qa.question: How much did the monetar base value increase to in 2013?
qa.context: The monetary base consists of coins and Federal Reserve Notes in circulation outside the Federal Reserve Banks and the U.S. Treasury, plus deposits held by depository institutions at Federal Reserve Banks. The adjusted monetary base has increased from approximately 400 billion dollars in 1994, to 800 billion in 2005, and over 3000 billion in 2013. The amount of cash in circulation is increased (or decreased) by the actions of the Federal Reserve System. Eight times a year, the 12-person Federal Open Market Committee meet to determine U.S. monetary policy. Every business day, the Federal Reserve System engages in Open market operations to carry out that monetary policy. If the Federal Reserve desires to increase the money supply, it will buy securities (such as U.S. Treasury Bonds) anonymously from banks in exchange for dollars. Conversely, it will sell securities to the banks in exchange for dollars, to take dollars out of circulation.
qa.encoded: By 2013, the adjusted monetary base jumped to over $3,000 billion (equivalently, more than $3 trillion). This was a remarkable increase from roughly $800 billion in 2005 and approximately $400 billion back in 1994. The growth was primarily driven by the Federal Reserve System's open market operations, through which the Fed purchases securities such as U.S. Treasury Bonds from banks, effectively injecting new dollars into circulation and expanding the monetary base significantly.

qa.question: What was one of the things the Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act do?
qa.context: In 1937 over 100 people died after ingesting "Elixir Sulfanilamide" manufactured by S.E. Massengill Company of Tennessee. The product was formulated in diethylene glycol, a highly toxic solvent that is now widely used as antifreeze. Under the laws extant at that time, prosecution of the manufacturer was possible only under the technicality that the product had been called an "elixir", which literally implied a solution in ethanol. In response to this episode, the U.S. Congress passed the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act of 1938, which for the first time required pre-market demonstration of safety before a drug could be sold, and explicitly prohibited false therapeutic claims.
qa.encoded: The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act of 1938 required, for the first time, that manufacturers demonstrate a drug's safety before it could be marketed and sold to the public. This pre-market safety requirement was a groundbreaking shift in pharmaceutical regulation. Earlier, there had been no obligation for companies to prove their products were safe prior to selling them. The Act also explicitly prohibited false therapeutic claims about drugs. Together, these provisions were a direct legislative response to the Elixir Sulfanilamide tragedy that killed over 100 people in 1937.

qa.question: What may a priest use in place of wine for consecration for health reasons?

Canon 924 of the Code of Canon Law specifies that the wine used for consecration must be natural and made from grapes of the vine. Grape juice, being derived from the same source, serves as the closest acceptable alternative. However, such dispensations are extremely rare and require sufficient justification to warrant them. The Church maintains strict guidelines to preserve the integrity and tradition of the Eucharistic celebration.

qa.question: What term describes the basic symmetries of the laws of mechanics?
qa.context: Lie groups are of fundamental importance in modern physics: Noether's theorem links continuous symmetries to conserved quantities. Rotation, as well as translations in space and time are basic symmetries of the laws of mechanics. They can, for instance, be used to construct simple models—imposing, say, axial symmetry on a situation will typically lead to significant simplification in the equations one needs to solve to provide a physical description.v[›] Another example are the Lorentz transformations, which relate measurements of time and velocity of two observers in motion relative to each other. They can be deduced in a purely group-theoretical way, by expressing the transformations as a rotational symmetry of Minkowski space. The latter serves—in the absence of significant gravitation—as a model of space time in special relativity. The full symmetry group of Minkowski space, i.e. including translations, is known as the Poincaré group. By the above, it plays a pivotal role in special relativity and, by implication, for quantum field theories. Symmetries that vary with location are central to the modern description of physical interactions with the help of gauge theory.
qa.encoded: Rotation, as well as translations in space and time, are the basic symmetries of the laws of mechanics. These exact symmetries underpin classical physics, allowing physicists to simplify complex problems by exploiting the invariance of mechanical laws under such transformations.

qa.question: What does 'everted' mean?
qa.context: Feeding structures in the mouth region vary widely, and have little correlation with the animals' diets. Many polychaetes have a muscular pharynx that can be everted (turned inside out to extend it). In these animals the foremost few segments often lack septa so that, when the muscles in these segments contract, the sharp increase in fluid pressure from all these segments everts the pharynx very quickly. Two families, the Eunicidae and Phyllodocidae, have evolved jaws, which can be used for seizing prey, biting off pieces of vegetation, or grasping dead and decaying matter. On the other hand, some predatory polychaetes have neither jaws nor eversible pharynges. Selective deposit feeders generally live in tubes on the sea-floor and use palps to find food particles in the sediment and then wipe them into their mouths. Filter feeders use "crowns" of palps covered in cilia that wash food particles towards their mouths. Non-selective deposit feeders ingest soil or marine sediments via mouths that are generally unspecialized. Some clitellates have sticky pads in the roofs of their mouths, and some of these can evert the pads to capture prey. Leeches often have an eversible proboscis, or a muscular pharynx with two or three teeth.
qa.encoded: The term "everted" means turned inside out in order to extend outward. Here, the passage itself clarifies this directly with a parenthetical definition: "turned inside out to extend it." Essentially, imagine pulling a glove or sock inside out so the interior lining becomes the exterior surface. This process in polychaetes is powered by muscular contractions in segments that lack internal dividers called septa, creating a sudden surge of fluid pressure that forces the pharynx outward at remarkable speed. As a feeding adaptation, eversion allows various annelid species to rapidly seize prey, bite off pieces of vegetation, or grasp decaying organic matter.

qa.question: What does splicing words affect?

For handheld appliances such as hair dryers, blenders, vacuum cleaners, and portable power tools like drills and sanders, this combination is essential — users need devices that are easy to hold and maneuver without sacrificing performance. The favorable power-to-weight ratio of universal motors makes them a natural fit for virtually any consumer product where compactness and vigorous high-speed operation matter most.

qa.question: Who completed the transepts and nave on Wells Cathedral in about 1230?
qa.context: At the Abbey Saint-Denis, Noyon Cathedral, Notre Dame de Paris and at the eastern end of Canterbury Cathedral in England, simple cylindrical columns predominate over the Gothic forms of clustered columns and shafted piers. Wells Cathedral in England, commenced at the eastern end in 1175, was the first building in which the designer broke free from Romanesque forms. The architect entirely dispensed with the round arch in favour of the pointed arch and with cylindrical columns in favour of piers composed of clusters of shafts which lead into the mouldings of the arches. The transepts and nave were continued by Adam Locke in the same style and completed in about 1230. The character of the building is entirely Gothic. Wells Cathedral is thus considered the first truly Gothic cathedral.
qa.encoded: Adam Locke completed the transepts and nave of Wells Cathedral in about 1230, carrying on the same purely Gothic design. Years prior, in 1175, construction had begun at the eastern end, but Locke maintained the distinctive style throughout—using pointed arches and clustered piers instead of round arches and cylindrical columns—making Wells what is widely considered the first truly Gothic cathedral.

qa.question: What are code points in the range U+DC00-U+DFFF known as?
qa.context: Code points in the range U+D800–U+DBFF (1,024 code points) are known as high-surrogate code points, and code points in the range U+DC00–U+DFFF (1,024 code points) are known as low-surrogate code points. A high-surrogate code point (also known as a leading surrogate) followed by a low-surrogate code point (also known as a trailing surrogate) together form a surrogate pair used in UTF-16 to represent 1,048,576 code points outside BMP. High and low surrogate code points are not valid by themselves. Thus the range of code points that are available for use as characters is U+0000–U+D7FF and U+E000–U+10FFFF (1,112,064 code points). The value of these code points (i.e., excluding surrogates) is sometimes referred to as the character's scalar value.
qa.encoded: Code points in the range U+DC00–U+DFFF are known as **low-surrogate code points** (also called trailing surrogates). This zone encompasses exactly 1,024 code points. A low-surrogate code point is not valid on its own—it must be preceded by a high-surrogate code point (from the range U+D800–U+DBFF) to form what is called a surrogate pair. Together, these surrogate pairs are utilized in UTF-16 encoding to represent 1,048,576 code points that lie outside the Basic Multilingual Plane (BMP).

qa.question: Which two major time periods did placentals appear?
qa.context: Recent molecular phylogenetic studies suggest that most placental orders diverged about 100 to 85 million years ago and that modern families appeared in the period from the late Eocene through the Miocene. But paleontologists object that no placental fossils have been found from before the end of the Cretaceous. The earliest undisputed fossils of placentals come from the early Paleocene, after the extinction of the dinosaurs. In particular, scientists have recently identified an early Paleocene animal named Protungulatum donnae as one of the first placental mammals. The earliest known ancestor of primates is Archicebus achilles from around 55 million years ago. This tiny primate weighed 20–30 grams (0.7–1.1 ounce) and could fit within a human palm.
qa.encoded: Based on the evidence presented, placental mammals are associated with two major time periods. Molecular phylogenetic studies suggest that most placental orders diverged during the **Cretaceous period**, approximately 100 to 85 million years ago. However, paleontologists challenge this, noting that no placental fossils have been found before the end of the Cretaceous. The earliest undisputed fossils actually come from the **early Paleocene**, just after the extinction of the dinosaurs. This juncture between the Cretaceous and Paleocene is critical to understanding placental mammal origins. Essentially, while genetic data points to a Cretaceous divergence, the fossil record firmly places their confirmed appearance in the Paleocene. Protungulatum donnae, identified from the early Paleocene, ranks among the first known placental mammals. So the two key periods are the late Cretaceous (based on molecular evidence) and the early Paleocene (based on fossil evidence).

qa.question: What kind of system is a solar chimney?
qa.context: A solar chimney (or thermal chimney, in this context) is a passive solar ventilation system composed of a vertical shaft connecting the interior and exterior of a building. As the chimney warms, the air inside is heated causing an updraft that pulls air through the building. Performance can be improved by using glazing and thermal mass materials in a way that mimics greenhouses.
qa.encoded: A solar chimney is a passive solar ventilation system designed to cool and ventilate buildings without relying on mechanical equipment. It operates through natural convection by using a vertical shaft that links the interior and exterior of a structure. As sunlight heats the chimney walls, the air inside warms and rises, producing an updraft that draws cooler, fresh air into the building from lower openings. Notable improvements in performance can be achieved by incorporating glazing and thermal mass materials, which trap and store heat in a manner similar to a greenhouse. This elegant approach harnesses solar energy to maintain comfortable indoor air quality entirely through passive means.

qa.question: How are the terms Hispanic and Latino not specific?
qa.context: The term "Hispanic" as an ethnonym emerged in the 20th century with the rise of migration of laborers from the Spanish-speaking countries of Latin America to the United States. The definitions of both terms are non-race specific, and include people who consider themselves to be of distinct races (Black, White, Amerindian, Asian, and mixed groups). However, there is a common misconception in the US that Hispanic/Latino is a race or sometimes even that national origins such as Mexican, Cuban, Colombian, Salvadoran, etc. are races. In contrast to "Latino" or "Hispanic", "Anglo" refers to non-Hispanic White Americans or non-Hispanic European Americans, most of whom speak the English language but are not necessarily of English descent.
qa.encoded: Notably, the definitions of both "Hispanic" and "Latino" are non-race specific, meaning they do not correspond to any single racial category. Individuals who identify with either term may consider themselves to be of entirely distinct races, including Black, White, Amerindian, Asian, or mixed racial groups. Neither label narrows identification down to one particular race or physical characteristic. This is often misunderstood in the United States, where many people mistakenly believe that Hispanic or Latino is itself a race, or even that specific national origins such as Mexican, Cuban, Colombian, or Salvadoran constitute separate races. Hence, both terms function purely as ethnic and cultural descriptors tied to Spanish-speaking heritage rather than as racial classifications.

qa.question: What mostly controls the rain in Mexico City?
qa.context: The area receives about 820 millimetres (32.3 in) of annual rainfall, which is concentrated from June through September/October with little or no precipitation the remainder of the year. The area has two main seasons. The rainy season runs from June to October when winds bring in tropical moisture from the sea. The dry season runs from November to May, when the air is relatively drier. This dry season subdivides into a cold period and a warm period. The cold period spans from November to February when polar air masses push down from the north and keep the air fairly dry. The warm period extends from March to May when tropical winds again dominate but do not yet carry enough moisture for rain.
qa.encoded: The rain in Mexico City is mostly governed by seasonal wind patterns that shift throughout the year. However, the primary driver is tropical moisture carried inland by winds from the sea. In the rainy season, spanning June through October, these moisture-laden tropical winds produce the vast majority of the city's roughly 820 millimetres of annual precipitation. Notably, during the dry season from November to May, polar air masses from the north and drier tropical currents suppress rainfall significantly. Key to understanding the overall precipitation cycle is recognizing that these alternating wind patterns — tropical versus polar — dictate when and whether enough moisture reaches the region to generate rain.

qa.question: What is the advantage of a coiled-coil filament over a straight filament?
qa.context: To improve the efficiency of the lamp, the filament usually consists of multiple coils of coiled fine wire, also known as a 'coiled coil'. For a 60-watt 120-volt lamp, the uncoiled length of the tungsten filament is usually 22.8 inches (580 mm), and the filament diameter is 0.0018 inches (0.046 mm). The advantage of the coiled coil is that evaporation of the tungsten filament is at the rate of a tungsten cylinder having a diameter equal to that of the coiled coil. The coiled-coil filament evaporates more slowly than a straight filament of the same surface area and light-emitting power. As a result, the filament can then run hotter, which results in a more efficient light source, while reducing the evaporation so that the filament will last longer than a straight filament at the same temperature.
qa.encoded: The coiled-coil filament provides a significant advantage over a straight filament in terms of both efficiency and longevity. The key reason is that tungsten evaporation from a coiled-coil occurs at the rate of a cylinder whose diameter matches that of the overall coil, rather than at the rate of the thin wire's actual surface area. This effectively means the coiled-coil filament evaporates much more slowly than a straight filament producing the same amount of light. Thanks to this reduced evaporation rate, the filament can be operated at a higher temperature, which produces more light per watt of electricity consumed. The hotter operating temperature directly translates to greater luminous efficiency. The filament also enjoys a longer operational lifespan compared to a straight filament running at the same temperature, since less tungsten is lost over time. Thus, the coiled-coil design achieves the best of both worlds — brighter, more efficient light output and improved durability.

qa.question: Why does basing sexual orientation on one component cause problems?
qa.context: As there is no research indicating which of the three components is essential in defining sexual orientation, all three are used independently and provide different conclusions regarding sexual orientation. Savin Williams (2006) discusses this issue and notes that by basing findings regarding sexual orientation on a single component, researchers may not actually capture the intended population. For example, if homosexual is defined by same sex behavior, gay virgins are omitted, heterosexuals engaging in same sex behavior for other reasons than preferred sexual arousal are miscounted, and those with same sex attraction who only have opposite-sex relations are excluded. Because of the limited populations that each component captures, consumers of research should be cautious in generalizing these findings.
qa.encoded: Basing sexual orientation on just one component is problematic because it inevitably excludes significant portions of the population researchers aim to study. Each of the three components—behavior, attraction, and identity—captures a different and limited subset of people. For example, if homosexuality is defined strictly by same-sex behavior, then gay individuals who haven't had sexual experiences are completely left out. Similarly, heterosexual people who engage in same-sex behavior for reasons other than preferred sexual arousal get incorrectly classified. Those who feel same-sex attraction but only pursue opposite-sex relationships are also missed under a behavior-based definition. Consumers of such research should therefore be cautious about generalizing findings, since any single-component approach produces an incomplete and potentially misleading picture of sexual orientation.

qa.question: What type of animal is most commonly used to study brain damage?
qa.context: Another approach to brain function is to examine the consequences of damage to specific brain areas. In humans, the effects of strokes and other types of brain damage have been a key source of information about brain function. Because there is no ability to experimentally control the nature of the damage, however, this information is often difficult to interpret. In animal studies, most commonly involving rats, it is possible to use electrodes or locally injected chemicals to produce precise patterns of damage and then examine the consequences for behavior.
qa.encoded: Rats are the most commonly used animals in studies of brain damage. As noted in the passage, researchers can use electrodes or locally injected chemicals to produce precise patterns of damage in rat brains, then examine the behavioral consequences. This experimental control is not possible with human subjects, which makes rats particularly valuable as a model organism for neuroscience.

qa.question: What creatures did Bard experiment on?
qa.context: Phillip Bard contributed to the theory with his work on animals. Bard found that sensory, motor, and physiological information all had to pass through the diencephalon (particularly the thalamus), before being subjected to any further processing. Therefore, Cannon also argued that it was not anatomically possible for sensory events to trigger a physiological response prior to triggering conscious awareness and emotional stimuli had to trigger both physiological and experiential aspects of emotion simultaneously.
qa.encoded: Bard experimented on animals as part of his research into the physiological basis of emotion. Keeping with the scientific approaches of his era, he studied how sensory, motor, and physiological information all passed through the diencephalon — particularly the thalamus — before any further processing occurred. His work demonstrated that emotional stimuli trigger both physiological and experiential aspects of emotion simultaneously, which became a central pillar of the Cannon-Bard theory.

qa.question: What business fields has Whitehead influenced?
qa.context: Whitehead has had some influence on philosophy of business administration and organizational theory. This has led in part to a focus on identifying and investigating the effect of temporal events (as opposed to static things) within organizations through an “organization studies” discourse that accommodates a variety of 'weak' and 'strong' process perspectives from a number of philosophers. One of the leading figures having an explicitly Whiteheadian and panexperientialist stance towards management is Mark Dibben, who works in what he calls "applied process thought" to articulate a philosophy of management and business administration as part of a wider examination of the social sciences through the lens of process metaphysics. For Dibben, this allows "a comprehensive exploration of life as perpetually active experiencing, as opposed to occasional – and thoroughly passive – happening." Dibben has published two books on applied process thought, Applied Process Thought I: Initial Explorations in Theory and Research (2008), and Applied Process Thought II: Following a Trail Ablaze (2009), as well as other papers in this vein in the fields of philosophy of management and business ethics.
qa.encoded: Overall, Whitehead has significantly influenced the philosophy of business administration and organizational theory, primarily through what Mark Dibben calls "applied process thought." One key contribution is a framework that views organizational life as perpetually active experiencing rather than passive happening, which has informed management philosophy and the study of temporal events within organizations. Other specific fields impacted by this Whiteheadian approach include business ethics and the broader social sciences, all examined through the lens of process metaphysics to better understand how organizations function as dynamic, evolving entities rather than static structures.

qa.question: What cathedral was damaged along with the medieval church of Temple Neuf?
qa.context: During the Franco-Prussian War and the Siege of Strasbourg, the city was heavily bombarded by the Prussian army. The bombardment of the city was meant to break the morale of the people of Strasbourg. On 24 and 26 August 1870, the Museum of Fine Arts was destroyed by fire, as was the Municipal Library housed in the Gothic former Dominican church, with its unique collection of medieval manuscripts (most famously the Hortus deliciarum), rare Renaissance books, archeological finds and historical artifacts. The gothic cathedral was damaged as well as the medieval church of Temple Neuf, the theatre, the city hall, the court of justice and many houses. At the end of the siege 10,000 inhabitants were left without shelter; over 600 died, including 261 civilians, and 3200 were injured, including 1,100 civilians.
qa.encoded: The gothic cathedral of Strasbourg was damaged along with the medieval church of Temple Neuf during the Prussian bombardment of the city. Very extensive destruction occurred throughout Strasbourg during the siege of August 1870, leaving thousands without shelter and causing hundreds of casualties among both soldiers and civilians.

qa.question: Who was George III trying to please with the Proclamation of 1763?
qa.context: The British government was close to bankruptcy, and Britain now faced the delicate task of pacifying its new French-Canadian subjects as well as the many American Indian tribes who had supported France. George III's Proclamation of 1763, which forbade white settlement beyond the crest of the Appalachians, was intended to appease the latter but led to considerable outrage in the Thirteen Colonies, whose inhabitants were eager to acquire native lands. The Quebec Act of 1774, similarly intended to win over the loyalty of French Canadians, also spurred resentment among American colonists. The act protected Catholic religion and French language, which enraged the Americans, but the Québécois remained loyal and did not rebel.
qa.encoded: George III was trying to appease the many American Indian tribes who had supported France during the war. The Proclamation of 1763 quite specifically forbade white settlement beyond the crest of the Appalachian Mountains, aiming to quell Native fears about losing their ancestral lands to colonial expansion. By acknowledging Indigenous territorial concerns, the Crown hoped to quietly restore order on the frontier after years of costly conflict.

qa.question: When a system is over-damped, what does it typically not experience?
qa.context: In DC circuits and pulsed circuits, current and voltage reversal are affected by the damping of the system. Voltage reversal is encountered in RLC circuits that are under-damped. The current and voltage reverse direction, forming a harmonic oscillator between the inductance and capacitance. The current and voltage will tend to oscillate and may reverse direction several times, with each peak being lower than the previous, until the system reaches an equilibrium. This is often referred to as ringing. In comparison, critically damped or over-damped systems usually do not experience a voltage reversal. Reversal is also encountered in AC circuits, where the peak current will be equal in each direction.
qa.encoded: An over-damped system typically does not experience a voltage reversal. The high level of damping causes the system to return to equilibrium smoothly, without oscillating or reversing direction. This is in contrast to under-damped systems, where ringing occurs and the voltage may reverse multiple times before settling down. Gradual decay toward equilibrium, rather than oscillation, is the defining characteristic of over-damped behavior.

qa.question: Who argued that drug regulators were greater serving the interests of the drug companies than the patients?
qa.context: Ben Goldacre has argued that regulators – such as the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) in the UK, or the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in the United States – advance the interests of the drug companies rather than the interests of the public due to revolving door exchange of employees between the regulator and the companies and friendships develop between regulator and company employees. He argues that regulators do not require that new drugs offer an improvement over what is already available, or even that they be particularly effective.
qa.encoded: Ben Goldacre made this argument. He contended that drug regulators, such as the MHRA in the UK and the FDA in the United States, were primarily advancing the interests of pharmaceutical companies rather than those of patients. Judging from his analysis, this problem largely stems from the revolving door phenomenon, where employees frequently move between regulatory agencies and the companies they oversee, fostering personal friendships that compromise objectivity. He further argued that regulators fail to require new drugs to offer genuine improvements over existing treatments or even to be particularly effective, which he saw as a fundamental failure of the regulatory system to protect public health.

qa.question: What religious denomination was the only one allowed to hold public office in NC until 1835?

qa.question: What term describes the group of integers related to a prime number?
qa.context: For any prime number p, there is also the multiplicative group of integers modulo p. Its elements are the integers 1 to p − 1. The group operation is multiplication modulo p. That is, the usual product is divided by p and the remainder of this division is the result of modular multiplication. For example, if p = 5, there are four group elements 1, 2, 3, 4. In this group, 4 · 4 = 1, because the usual product 16 is equivalent to 1, which divided by 5 yields a remainder of 1. for 5 divides 16 − 1 = 15, denoted
qa.encoded: The term that describes the group of integers related to a prime number is the **multiplicative group of integers modulo p**. Naturally, this group is composed of the integers from 1 to p − 1, where the group operation is multiplication modulo p — meaning you multiply two elements and then take the remainder after dividing by p. Every element in this group possesses a multiplicative inverse, which is what qualifies it as a group in the algebraic sense. For example, when p = 5, the group elements are {1, 2, 3, 4}, and operations like 4 · 4 yield 1 because 16 mod 5 equals 1.
